---
title: "Data, Evidence, and Decision Making (with R)"
author: "Ayush Patel"
subtitle: "Session 2: Getting Started with R"
institute: "National Law School of India University, Bengaluru"
date: today
date-format: "DD MMM, YYYY"
embed-resources: true
bibliography: citations.bib
execute: 
  echo: true
format: 
  revealjs:
    fig-height: 6
    fig-width: 14
    embed-resources: true
    margin-left: 50px
    margin-right: 50px
    slide-number: c/t
    width: 1400
    height: 850
    theme: [default, custom.scss]
    footer: "Email: ayush.ap58@gmail.com"
---

# Readings 

The required readings before this class:

1. Chapter 2, *Get Started* of @healy2019socviz
2. Chapter 2, *Workflow: basics* of @wickham2023r4ds

## You are expected to know

1. What is R and RStudio
2. How to open RStudio
3. Create a project in Studio
4. What is a console?
5. Basics of objects and simple operations
6. Function: how to use
7. What is a script
8. What is a quarto doc


## This Session

1. I will take you through some simple code exercises
2. Explore objects
3. Basics of subsetting
4. Get comfortable running code and reading output and errors.

# The Basics

## R as a Calculator

```{r}
2+2
```


```{r}
2*5
```


## Comparing Values

```{r}
5 > 5

2^5 < 4025

"ayush" == "ayush" ## Notice any thing ?

(5 > 4) & (5 > 6)

(5 > 4) | (5 > 6)

```

## Storing Information

```{r}
# Assigning values

a <- 5

6 -> b

c = 10

d <- "will this guy even teach us the good stuff?"

a
b
c
d
```


## Remember

1. Mathematical commands in R are written in *almost* the same way as you would write in a piece of paper.

2. =  and == are **not the same** thing.

3. Values/information can be stored. These things that store values can be given names you like. These are called objects.

4. <- or -> or = can be used to store information/values. **Please use <- or ->**.


## Activity 1 

:::: {.columns}


::: {.column width="20%"}  

![Gabbar does not like to exercise](https://media1.giphy.com/media/v1.Y2lkPTc5MGI3NjExNDRqMGZhZ3VtODJ1Y3BxOTNqOG52MWoyOHpyMGloMjMxanhicjVwbiZlcD12MV9pbnRlcm5hbF9naWZfYnlfaWQmY3Q9Zw/26n60zk988k2wFoSk/giphy.gif)

:::

::: {.column width="80%"}

Copy the code on the screen. Follow the instructions and complete this code.

```{r}
#| eval=FALSE

# Store our name in the object called "name"
name <- 

# create an object that stores your birth year in YYYY format,
# name it "y_birth"
 
   
# Run this line of code after you have completed the above two lines of code

message(
  if(1975>y_birth){
    paste(1975-y_birth,"years after you were born, the film Sholya was released",
          sep = " ")
  }else{
    paste(
      "Sholay was released",
      y_birth - 1975,
      "years before you were born."
    )
  }
)
    
```


:::

::::

# Object: A brief overview

## Assignment Operator

`<-` or `->` are used to assign values to Object.
<br>

`=` works as well, I prefer `->` or `<-`

```{r}
a <- 2
b <- "Ayush"
c(1,25,53,5) -> obj
a
b
obj
```

## Type

```{r}
typeof(a)
typeof(b)
typeof(obj)
```

## class - vectors

```{r}
class(a)
class(b)
class(obj)
```

## class - data frames

```{r}
penguins
```


## class - list

```{r}
ex_list <- list(
    a,b,obj,penguins
)

ex_list
```

## Creating objects -atomic vectors

Such objects can be created by using the `c()` function.

```{r}
c(1,2,3,8,6)

fictional_characters <- c("Thor", "Gimli", "Frodo")

fictional_characters
```

These kind of objects are **homogeneous**. *What does that even mean??*

```{r }
info_store1 <- c("ayush", 854, "hello")

info_store2 <- c("ayush", "854", "hello")

info_store1

info_store2
```

## Creating objects - A dataframe

A data-frame, by it self, *can have values that are of different types*, i.e. numeric, character, date, etc...<br>
**But, the columns (vectors) that make a data frame are homogeneous.**

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column}

Base R approach
```{r}
data.frame(
  movie = c("Sholay", "LoTR", "Lathe Joshi"),
  year_release = c(1975,2001,2016)
) -> obj1

obj1
```


:::

::: {.column}

tidy approach
```{r}
library(tibble)
tibble(
  movie = c("Sholay", "LoTR", "Lathe Joshi"),
  year_release = c(1975,2001,2016)
)-> obj2

obj2
```


:::

::::

## Creating objects - A list

Lists are created using the `list()` functions.

**These are heterogeneous objects.**

```{r}
collected_info <- list(fictional_characters, "Ayush", c(32,5,4), obj2)

collected_info
```

# Extract from objects

## How to Extract


:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="30%"}

![You trying to extract from objects](https://media3.giphy.com/media/v1.Y2lkPTc5MGI3NjExY2l3dmV5aHQ2MXB6Zjlidjl2OTJqaTlkb2Zpb2Z0Mmh2YmMxaThyeSZlcD12MV9pbnRlcm5hbF9naWZfYnlfaWQmY3Q9Zw/PlnQNcQ4RYOhG/giphy.gif)

:::

::: {.column width="70%"}

### There can be three ways to get desired parts of an object

1. by referring to the exact .big[place or position] within an object.

2. by using the .big[name of the part] you want to access.

3. by providing a .big[condition] to access only those parts that fulfill this condition.


:::

::::

## From Atomic Vectors

```{r}
#| include: false
random_number <- rnorm(50,25,7)

month_names <- month.name
```

```{r}
random_number

month_names
```

## From Atomic Vectors
<br><br>
**By Position**

```{r}
random_number[1]

random_number[c(1,5,8)]

month_names[-5]

month_names[-c(1,10)]
```

## From Atomic Vectors
<br><br>
**Using a condition**

```{r}
random_number[random_number>20]

month_names[month_names == "June"]
```

## From a Dataframe

```{r}
penguins
```

## From a Dataframe

**By position**

```{r}
penguins[5,7]

penguins[c(1,3),c(2,8)]
```

## From a Dataframe

**Using a condition**

```{r}
names(penguins) # Also, this function gives us all the column names in a data frame
```

```{r}
penguins$bill_len # the '$' operator allows us to select a column of the data frame
```

```{r}
penguins[penguins$body_mass >3100,c("bill_len", "year")]
```

## From a List

```{r echo=FALSE}
list_obj <- list(fictional_characters,random_number,obj1,penguins)

```

```{r}
list_obj
```

## From a List

**By Position**

```{r}
list_obj[1]

list_obj[[1]]

typeof(list_obj[1])

typeof(list_obj[[1]]) # What do you see?
```

```{r}
list_obj[[4]][c(1:10),c(1:5)] # Notice anything??
```

## From a List

**Using a condition**

```{r}
list_obj[[2]][list_obj[[2]]>15]
```

# Activity 2
Copy and execute the following code.

```{r eval=FALSE}
install.packages("palmerpenguins") # dont run this if already installed

library(palmerpenguins) # dont run if already loaded

collected_data <- list(
  rnorm(25,51,6.5),
  month.abb,
  penguins
)

```

Write code to:

1. call the object `collected_data`

2. Subset only those values from the first element of the list `collected_data` that are greater than 18

3. Subset the list `collected_data` so that you get only those observations where the penguin is of the species gentoo and weighs more than 3350

# Missing Values

![Not finding stuff when expected](https://media3.giphy.com/media/v1.Y2lkPTc5MGI3NjExNHIxYXU3Mzc3ZmRtemRpMjB0MzY3eGUydm1vcnN1eTdvZTdrNm5iZCZlcD12MV9pbnRlcm5hbF9naWZfYnlfaWQmY3Q9Zw/26hkhPJ5hmdD87HYA/giphy.gif)

## Missing Values - What?

An experiment where people are asked time exactly at noon and record the answers. Interest is to find variance of the observations.

**Person A:** Hey! Could you tell me What is the time?
<br>
**Person B:** Sorry! I am not wearing a watch or carrying my phone.

|Recorded time| difference from actual time|
|-------------|----------------------------|
|12:00|0|
|12:05|5|
|12:10|10|
|11:50|-10|
| - | - |
|12:01|1|
|12:09|9|

## Missing Values - Identification


:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="30%"}

![Identify Missing values](https://media3.giphy.com/media/v1.Y2lkPTc5MGI3NjExdTNsMWRsZGUwN284YWRzem0wemE5dzg0dmdqajE3Y2syNTZrZ2dwMCZlcD12MV9pbnRlcm5hbF9naWZfYnlfaWQmY3Q9Zw/26DMUXQypGJaHhTsQ/giphy.gif)

:::

::: {.column}

Using the function `is.na()`.

Returns `TRUE` or `FALSE` depending the value in an object.

```{r}
is.na(NA)
is.na(c(0,5,10,-10,NA,1,9))
a <- c("ayush","saswata","piyush", NA)
is.na(a)
```


:::

::::

## Missing Values - Troubles


:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="30%"}

![Ruins perfectly good stuff](https://media3.giphy.com/media/v1.Y2lkPTc5MGI3NjExamQ0bnczcWk4b2JvdW0zaDY1OWphejBzaWZmM3hjMjh6empqeTB4ayZlcD12MV9pbnRlcm5hbF9naWZfYnlfaWQmY3Q9Zw/26CaMOXjjDM3Xl2s8/giphy.gif)

:::

::: {.column}

Not considering NAs in your data can generate erroneous results

```{r}
5+ NA
c(1,2,3,5,8,6,NA,2,58) -> vals
vals + 1
vals - 1
vals * 2
```


:::

::::

# Functions

## What are Functions

  + A fixed set of operation or commands. All these commands get executed if the **function is called**.

  + Expects specific types of inputs, *usually*, to generate outputs. These inputs are referred to as **arguments**

  + Has a name. Using this name a function can be called.

  + You can make your own functions too. 
  
  + Functions are objects.

## Why Use Functions

No need to copy and paste code again and again.  
Less chances of errors due to reduced copy paste.  
Easier to maintain or update  


::::: {.columns}

::: {.column}

![Without Functions](https://media4.giphy.com/media/v1.Y2lkPTc5MGI3NjExYXFxeWVicmU0c2sxYTR5YTNienB2cXhkaWxmY3cyZHJ5OHA1N281bSZlcD12MV9pbnRlcm5hbF9naWZfYnlfaWQmY3Q9Zw/l0HUbCY5FyDDTCire/giphy.gif)

:::

::: {.column}

![Using Functions](https://media2.giphy.com/media/v1.Y2lkPTc5MGI3NjExMmdzb2pzNjhuaTk5Znd2N2xmY3hyb2JxaW9hbHk0MXN1NG12Y2tsZCZlcD12MV9pbnRlcm5hbF9naWZfYnlfaWQmY3Q9Zw/lkhfMijDDuSQM/giphy.gif)

:::

::::

## Functions- how to use

```{r}
c(1,2,3,5.5,7,5,2,3,NA) -> a
c("ayush","dobby","Gimli") -> b
list(a,b) -> store_list
length(a)
length(store_list)
```

## Some Essentials {.smaller}


:::: {.columns}

::: {.column}

### Length

```{r}
length(a)
length(b)
```

### Unique

```{r}
unique(b)
```


### Mean/Median

```{r}
mean(a)
mean(a, na.rm = T)
median(a, na.rm = T)
```


:::

::: {.column}

### Ifelse

```{r}
ifelse(
  a > 3,
  "Greater than 3",
  "Not Greater than 3"
)
ifelse(
  a > 3,
  a - 1,
  a*0
)
```

### Sum

```{r}
sum(a)
sum(a, na.rm = TRUE)
```

:::

::::


## Some Essentials

### names

```{r}
names(penguins)
```

### dim

```{r}
dim(penguins)
```

## Activity 3

Write code to get:  

1) The mean of the first element of the list `collected_data`  

2) The number of rows of the third element of the object `collected_data`  

3) The all the unique values the variable `species` can take   

4) The length of the second element of the list `collected data`  

5) view the penguins data from the object `collected_Data`  

# End of Session 2

# References
